query: evidence on immunoglobulins in oral cavity defense

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Mucosal Lesions, Immunologic Diseases -- Etiology -- Vesiculobullous Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Vesiculobullous diseases are a group of immune-modulated conditions that often affect the oral cavity; the most discussed vesiculobullous diseases include mucous membrane pemphigoid and pemphigus vulgaris (see Image. Vesticobullous Disease). These diseases are both B-cell–mediated autoimmune conditions characterized by antibody response toward the structural components of the epithelium, resulting in the formation of vesicles and bullae on the oral mucosa and other mucosal and skin surfaces. Characteristically, mucous membrane pemphigoid demonstrates immunoglobulin G (IgG), immunoglobulin A, immunoglobulin M, or complement factor 3 autoantibodies against basement membrane components, resulting in complete separation of the mucosa (epidermis) from the submucosa (dermis) and causing subepithelial bullae. [6] [7] Pemphigus vulgaris is typically characterized by IgG autoantibodies directed against desmosomes, resulting in loss of cell-to-cell adhesion in the mucosa (epidermis) that can progress to intraepithelial vesicles (see Image. Pemphigus Vulgaris). [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Palatine Tonsil (Faucial Tonsils) -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Located bilaterally on the lateral aspect of the oropharynx and near the entrance of the gastrointestinal and upper respiratory tracts, the palatine tonsils come into contact with various inhaled or ingested pathogens and other materials that may gain exposure to the body through the mouth.  As a mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, the palatine tonsils serve as the primary lymphatic tissue of the oropharynx. [2] This tissue houses B cells that can go through the maturation process and produce all isotypes of immunoglobulins (IgA, IgD, IgE, IgG, and IgM). [2] Recent reseacrh demonstrates that, like the thymus, the tonsils have been shown to express T cell developmental intermediates that resemble those found in the thymus and bone marrow. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Immunoglobulin -- Function -- Immunoglobulin A
retrieved_docs.content: IgA appears in 2 different molecular structures: monomeric (serum) and dimeric structure (secretory). The serum IgA has a molecular weight of 160 Kd and a serum concentration of 3 mg/mL. Secretory IgA (sIgA) has a molecular weight of 385 Kd and a mean serum concentration of 0.05 mg/mL. IgA is the major antibody in secretions found in saliva, tears, colostrum, intestinal, genital tract, and respiratory secretions.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Mucosal Lesions, Immunologic Diseases -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the most common oral mucosal lesions of immune-mediated disease and their etiology. Differentiate common physical exam findings of oral mucosal lesions of immune-mediated disease. Apply the appropriate treatment modalities for the most common oral mucosal lesions of immune-mediated disease. Communicate and collaborate effectively with interprofessional team members to obtain the best treatment outcomes for oral mucosal lesions of immune-mediated disease. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Parotitis -- Pathophysiology -- Inflammatory and Autoimmune Causes
retrieved_docs.content: A viral infection may trigger the initial insult that leads to autoimmune parotitis. Epithelial cells of the parotid gland take up autoantigens or viral antigens bound in antigen-antibody complexes. These complexes interact with class II histocompatibility molecules in the cytoplasm, prompting the expression of the human leukocyte antigen complex on the cell surface for presentation to T cells. CD4+ cells recognize antigens on the cell surface and release cytokines, thereby triggering additional T-cell activation. Subsequently, B cells enter the gland and secrete autoantibodies, such as anti-Sjögren syndrome type A antigen (anti-SSA), anti-Sjögren syndrome type B antigen (anti-SSB), and rheumatoid factor, resulting in oligoclonal expansion and acinar destruction that can increase the risk of neoplastic transformation. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Preventing Cross Infection in the Dental Office -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Postexposure management for hepatitis B virus (HBV) involves providing multiple doses of hepatitis B immune globulin and the hepatitis B vaccine. [14] Hepatitis B immunoglobulin is a human immunoglobulin used to prevent the development of hepatitis B after acute exposure to the surface antigen of the hepatitis B virus (HBsAg). [15] The level of antibodies of the exposed individual should be tested if the source is positive for HBsAg. If the exposed person is not immune (has never been immunized, did not seroconvert to the vaccine, or has antibody levels to HBsAg less than 10 mIU/mL), the treatment is to give a single dose of hepatitis B immunoglobulin between 48 to 72 hours of the exposure and to start a course of HBV vaccination. [14] The HBV vaccine should be given within 7 days of exposure, 1 or 2 months after, and then 6 months after the first dose. The level of immunity should be tested 2 to 4 weeks later. The risk of transmission of HBV is more than 30% if the source is positive and post-exposure prophylaxis has not been used. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Antibody -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: IgM is the first antibody produced, therefore it is the primary antibody response to pathogens. It is secreted form exists as a pentamer that allows strong binding of the Ig to the pathogen at the initial encounter. IgG is the most abundant antibody present in the serum. It functions to fix complement, neutralize toxins, and opsonizes bacteria. IgG is the only Ig that crosses the placenta and allows protection to the developing infant until levels decline substantially 6 months after birth. IgA mediates immune response in the mucosal membranes such as the gastrointestinal and respiratory lining. This antibody is also present in secretions such as tears and saliva as well as breast milk. IgE is used to mediate the type 1 hypersensitivity reaction in response to allergens with the use of inflammatory mediators such as bradykinins and histamines. IgE does this by binding on the surface of mast cells and basophils. The function of IgD is not entirely understood. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Mumps -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Mumps laboratory confirmation techniques include reverse transcriptase-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) and serum immunoglobulin M (IgM) antibodies. At the initial presentation of an individual suspected of mumps infection, the treating clinician should collect 2 specimens: a buccal or oral swab for RT-PCR and an acute-phase serum specimen for IgM and IgG antibodies. Ideally, buccal specimens should be obtained within 3 days of parotid gland swelling and no later than 8 days after symptom onset. Massaging the parotid gland 30 seconds before buccal swab collection can improve specimen quality. The IgM response may not be detectable for up to 5 days after the onset of symptoms in unvaccinated individuals and may not be detectable in vaccinated people. [1] Incorrect collection of acute-phase samples can lead to false-negative results. When this occurs, repeat serum samples should be collected 7 to 10 days after the onset of symptoms to yield positive results. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Angular Chelitis -- Etiology -- Manifestations of Systemic Diseases - Sjogren Syndrome, Inflammatory Bowel Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Sjogren syndrome (SS): AC is the most common oral lesion found in SS, followed by atrophic glossitis and oral candidiasis. This is according to a systematic review by Serrano et al., which incorporated the data of 2426 patients with SS. The prevalence of AC ranged from 2% to 81%, with the largest population reporting a frequency of 20-40%. SS is a rheumatologic disease characterized by xerostomia ("dry mouth") and hyposialia ("decreased salivation"). This results from lymphocytic infiltration and destruction of salivary glands. Surprisingly, in this patient population, there is an inverse relationship between salivary flow and the presence of candidiasis, which is the opposite of what is the case for non-Sjogren’s AC. This may be explained by proper levels of saliva allowing for mucosal lubrication, tissue healing, and local immunity. Salivary IgA inhibits the binding of candida species to mucosal surfaces and flushes Candida from the oral cavity. Dentures still predispose SS patients to AC since dental orthotics act as a reservoir for Candida and is a risk factor for oral candidiasis. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Salivary Glands -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Human salivary glands produce between 0.5 to 1.5 L of saliva daily, facilitating mastication, swallowing, and speech, lubricating the oral mucosa, and providing an aqueous medium for taste perception. They also participate in the digestion of triglycerides and starches by secreting lipases and amylases. In addition, saliva plays a protective role against infections via its many organic constituents. These include the secretory piece, a glycoprotein that forms a complex with immunoglobulin A (IgA) to defend against viruses and bacteria, lysozymes that cause bacterial agglutination, autolysin to degrade bacterial cell walls, and lactoferrin to sequester iron (an element vital to bacterial growth) [2] . Additionally, saliva contains ionic compounds, such as bicarbonates, that buffer acids produced by bacteria and protect the oral cavity and esophagus from gastric juice. As a result, saliva plays a vital role in protecting the mouth from chronic buccal mucosal infections and dental caries [3] .

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Opsonization -- Mechanism -- Antibodies
retrieved_docs.content: Antibodies are part of the adaptive immune system and are produced by plasma cells in response to a specific antigen. Different antigens stimulate different B cells to develop into plasma cells. An antibody’s complex structure enables its specificity to certain antigens. At the end of the light and heavy chains, antibodies have variable regions, also known as antigen-binding sites. These sites allow the antibody to fit like “a lock and key” into the epitopes of specific antigens. Once the antigen-binding sites are bound to the epitopes on the antigen, the stem region of the antibody binds to the receptor on the phagocytes. Multiple antibodies bind to multiple sites on the antigen, increasing the chance and efficiency in which the pathogen is engulfed in the phagosome and destroyed by lysosomes. Immunoglobulin A (IgA) often exists in a monomeric state when in the bloodstream but it is secreted into the lumen of mucosal surfaces in a dimeric form. The dimeric form is joined together by a "J chain" with two antigen-binding sites for the neutralization of pathogens. IgA can be found in saliva, tears, respiratory, gastrointestinal, and genitourinary secretions. Immunoglobulin M (IgM), on the other hand, exists as a pentamer joined by a "J chain" when secreted into the bloodstream. Its response to pathogen invasion is mostly swift while that of Immunoglobulin G is delayed. When IgM is affixed on the surface of B cells, it exists as a monomer. Immunoglobulin G (IgG) is produced by plasma cells after class switching of antibodies. It can also cross the placenta and establish passive immunity. Immunoglobulin D (IgD) is mainly found bound to B cells. [2] Immunoglobulin E (IgE) activates mast cells and basophils leading to the release and formation of inflammatory mediators such as histamine, carboxypeptidase A3, tryptase, eosinophil chemotactic factor, prostaglandin D2, Leukotrienes, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and platelet-activating factor (PAF). IgE is involved in allergic reactions and immune response to helminths and protozoan pathogens. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Salivary Glands -- Clinical Significance -- Conclusion
retrieved_docs.content: Human salivary glands represent a crucial part of oropharyngeal anatomy. Saliva contains amylase, which initiates digestion through the breakdown of carbohydrates. Salivary gland pathology may be a harbinger of systemic disease, including conditions that may shorten the life span. These glands also play a crucial role in immunologic defense as saliva contains many immunoglobins, mainly IgA, which help defend the oropharynx from microbes. Ultrasound is the gold standard for imaging of salivary glands. Histologic findings of salivary glands can be imperative in determining a diagnosis, and biopsies should be considered, especially in the setting of chronic autoimmune or inflammatory conditions. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Mucosal Lesions, Immunologic Diseases -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity reviews the etiology, evaluation, identification, and management of the commonly discussed immune-mediated mucosal lesions of the oral cavity. The role of the multidisciplinary healthcare team involved in treating patients with these conditions is discussed.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Tonsils -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Like other lymphoid tissues, the tonsils contribute to immune defense by responding to infections and foreign antigens. The tonsils are strategically located at the junction of the respiratory and digestive tracts, where they initiate lymphokine and immunoglobulin production in response to inhaled or ingested pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Microbiology is identified as an applicable qualifier for the oral conditions قيلة مثانية, قيلة مستقيمية, and اضطراب اللسان, indicating that microbiological factors are relevant in the assessment or management of these disorders. Bacterial vaccines are, in turn, an applicable qualifier for microbiology, reflecting the role of immunization strategies within the broader microbiological context. The term “Without” is noted as not being associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, suggesting that the absence of a particular factor does not have a direct relationship with these specific conditions. Together, these connections highlight the importance of microbiological considerations and vaccine‑related interventions in certain oral pathologies, while clarifying that “Without” does not contribute to their etiology or presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: disorders are placed under the broader category of blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم), indicating that globulin deficiency underlies or is related to various immune system abnormalities. Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1727
retrieved_docs.content: Thyreoglobulin and omalizumab are recognized as permissible qualifiers in the context of ultrastructure, meaning that both of these agents may be used to label or characterize ultrastructural features in microscopic examinations. This allowance indicates that researchers and clinicians can employ thyreoglobulin, a thyroid hormone precursor protein, and omalizumab, an anti‑IgE monoclonal antibody, as specific markers when assessing cellular and subcellular architecture, thereby enhancing the precision of ultrastructural analysis.

Beklomethason is indicated for generic inflammation (الالْتِهاب), and aspiration pneumonitis is mapped to respiratory inflammation, illustrating overlapping inflammatory pathways across organ systems. Generic inflammation is directly related to respiratory inflammation, and other bone diseases and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with both generic inflammation and bone resorption, reflecting the interplay between inflammation and skeletal degradation. Oral diseases (excluding dental conditions) are organized under salivary‑gland dysfunction, reflecting anatomical connections. Blood coagulation factors are contraindicated with tranexamic acid, indicating a therapeutic restriction to avoid adverse coagulation effects. Other central nervous system infections and poliomyelitis are subcategories of bulbar inflammation, emphasizing their involvement of the brainstem bulbar region. Finally, other connective tissue

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2108

Omalizumab, a monoclonal anti‑IgE antibody, is linked to metabolism and urine as qualifiers, highlighting its impact on allergic inflammation, metabolic aspects of immune activation, and potential urinary biomarkers. Thyroglobulin, the thyroid autoantigen essential for thyroid‑hormone synthesis, qualifies metabolism, urine, omalizumab, puberty (البلوغ), multiple myeloma, and peptides, reflecting its involvement in autoimmune thyroid disease, metabolic regulation, and as a measurable urinary marker. Puberty (البلوغ) and multiple myeloma are metabolic qualifiers and urinary qualifiers, representing developmental metabolic remodeling and malignant plasma‑cell disease with dysregulated immune function, bone and protein metabolism, and detectable urinary changes. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 881
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations represent a broad category of therapeutic interventions that encompass immunization, injections, and perfusion. Within this overarching group, human vaccination is a specific subclass, and it further includes distinct forms such as immunization for autoimmune disease and passive immunization (التمنيع السلبي). Passive immunization, in turn, comprises interventions like the injection of RhD immune globulin, which is recognized as a type of passive immunization. Additionally, prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified both as a therapeutic intervention (under immunization, injections, and perfusion) and as a component of the larger prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations category. This hierarchical organization highlights how various vaccination and immunization strategies are interrelated, ranging from general preventive measures to specialized applications for autoimmune conditions and passive immune protection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2570
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease is organized hierarchically to include human vaccination, and human vaccination in turn encompasses التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Injection of RhD immune globulin represents a specific form of التمنيع السلبي, illustrating that this RhD immune‑globulin injection is an example of passive immunization used within the broader context of human vaccination strategies for autoimmune disease prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease includes human vaccination as a subordinate concept, indicating that human vaccination is regarded as a specific form of immunization employed to prevent or modulate autoimmune disease in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1591
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease encompasses human vaccination as a specific form of immunization, indicating that vaccines administered to humans can be used to prevent or modulate autoimmune conditions. Human vaccination is situated within the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, reflecting its role as a preventive intervention designed to protect individuals from infectious agents. Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions as the overarching classification that groups together all preventive vaccine strategies, including human vaccination, and thereby provides the hierarchical framework linking general preventive immunization concepts to the more specific application of immunization for autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified as a therapeutic intervention and is a subcategory of the broader group termed Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This relationship indicates that preventive vaccination against bacterial pathogens is considered part of the overall therapeutic strategies that include various forms of immunization, injectable treatments, and perfusion techniques, highlighting its role within comprehensive patient care aimed at preventing infection.
